dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 responding to an allergic reaction at a restaurant on Broad Street.
Hi, I'm Alex, a Paramedic with the rescue squad. Can you tell me what's going on with your wife?
She had an allergic reaction after eating a pepper. She has asthma.
She's in severe respiratory distress with stridor, audible wheezing, flushed face, and hives.
She responds only with head nods or shakes. Her GCS is 12-E4, V2, M6.
Her initial vitals: blood pressure 144/106, pulse 125, respiratory rate 24, SpO2 98%.
We couldn't collect a glucose reading initially due to the patient's condition.
She's already used two EpiPens-right and left thighs.
Starting a Duoneb nebulizer (Albuterol and Atrovent) with O2 at 8 LPM.
Capnography is being utilized to monitor her CO2 levels continuously.
Placing a 4-lead ECG for cardiac monitoring.
Starting an IV in her left arm using an 18-gauge needle with saline lock.
Administering Benadryl 50mg intravenously for allergic reaction management.
Vitals are better now: pulse 118, respiratory rate 20, blood pressure 122/83, SpO2 99%.
She's still agitated. Airway is slightly swollen and red. Wheezing and stridor are less intense, but lower breath sounds are weak.
She does not report any pain at this time.
Before we arrived, she used two Albuterol puffs and two EpiPens.
She's calmer, and her breathing is better, but she's still in distress. Transport will be closely monitored.
She has a history of moderate asthma.
She has hives and a flushed face. No rashes or abrasions noted.
Her airway shows no obstruction or drainage.
Her neck is supple. No nuchal rigidity or lymphadenopathy.
Breath sounds are diminished in the bases due to wheezing.
Her abdomen is soft, non-tender, and unremarkable.
No signs of a urinary tract infection or rectal bleeding.
Extremities move adequately and the IV site is patent.
She is not reporting any pain at this time.
Placing her in Fowler's position to support breathing for transport.
She is secured using all straps from the stair chair to the ambulance stretcher.
She remains in respiratory distress, but symptoms are less dramatic now.
Transport is hot due to her ongoing need for monitoring and airway support.
The patient is secured with all straps in Fowler's position using the wheelchair transport system.
VCU Health Systems is our destination for definitive care.
We are following protocol 3-3 for medical - allergic reaction/anaphylaxis.
Her vitals remain stable during the ongoing transport preparations.
The Duoneb was effective in reducing stridor and wheezing, but she is still tachypneic.
She is on O2 at 8 LPM and has good oxygen saturation.
Transport is hot to ensure continuous airway and vital monitoring.
All straps are used to secure the patient in the correct position for transport.
We are transporting her to VCU Health Systems for emergency care.
During transport, she had a wet but unproductive cough.
The patient is transferred to the stretcher and secured for final transport.
VCU Health Systems remains our destination for emergency care.
